THE RISE REPORT
YOUR WEEKLY READ ON THE REFEREE WORLD
Week of June 22, 2026
Rise officials at the 2026 MLS NEXT Cup.  Photo: Rise Officials

📰 Around the World

A World Cup on Home Soil, Refereed Out Loud

The 2026 World Cup is being played in our own backyard, and the officiating story has been every bit as big as the goals. FIFA appointed its largest-ever slate — 52 referees, 88 assistant referees, and 30 video match officials — across the expanded 48-team field. A record eight US referees were named, the second-most of any country. Ismail Elfath led the first all-American crew of the tournament in Netherlands 2–2 Japan, and Tori Penso made history in Atlanta as the first American woman — and only the second woman ever — to referee a men's World Cup match, in Czechia 1–1 South Africa. Seeing officials we've learned from working the world's biggest stage is something to be proud of.

The new and expanded laws have been on full display. Paraguay's Miguel Almirón became the first player ever sent off under FIFA's new mouth-covering rule, dismissed by referee Iván Barton against Türkiye. Expanded VAR produced another first in USA–Paraguay, where Danny Makkelie went to the monitor to rescind a yellow and instead booked Almirón for simulation. And in Canada's 6–0 win over Qatar, two Qatar red cards came through VAR — including an upgraded caution for the tackle that broke Ismael Koné's leg, a sobering reminder of what rides on every challenge we judge.

⭐ Rise Spotlight

From the Generation adidas Cup: a FIFA instructor from Concacaf delivering instruction to a FIFA referee and our Rise Officials referees. This is development at the highest level — the kind of one-on-one coaching and feedback that turns good officials into great ones.

📅 The Week Ahead

The group stage is nearly done, and the knockouts come fast. This 48-team World Cup introduces a brand-new Round of 32 — the first any World Cup has ever staged — running June 28–July 3: 90 minutes, 30 of extra time, then kicks from the mark if level. There are still bracket-deciding group games, including Ismail Elfath's second assignment, Uruguay v Spain (Group H, June 26). Coaching point for Rise referees: review your kicks-from-the-mark mechanics now, before you need them — your pre-shootout checklist (the ends and which goal, the coin toss for order, eligible players only with reduce-to-equate, the keeper staying on the line until the kick, and clear tracking with your ARs and 4th official). Knockout soccer is decided on details — so is knockout officiating.
